does: This paper introduces Parenting, an algorithm for safe reinforcement learning that leverages human input during training to mitigate core safety failures. The method is inspired by how human parents guide children through learning, using human judgment to constrain exploration and prevent reward hacking, side effects, and other unsafe behaviors. The authors demonstrate that Parenting successfully solves the AI Safety gridworld benchmarks from Leike et al., show that agents can eventually surpass their human supervisors' performance as training progresses, and establish that policies learned this way transfer to new environments beyond their training distribution.
connects: This work extends prior research on safe reinforcement learning and human-in-the-loop AI training. It builds on formal safety frameworks that identify pathological RL behaviors like reward hacking and negative side effects, and responds to the observation that human oversight during learning can prevent these failures. The paper connects to human feedback approaches and imitation learning traditions, while engaging with Leike et al.'s systematic characterization of RL safety problems. It positions human guidance not as a permanent crutch but as a training signal that allows agents to eventually exceed human capability while remaining safe.
enables: This framework opens pathways for deploying autonomous agents in high-stakes domains where human experts can provide real-time correction without fully scripting behavior. It suggests that hybrid human-agent learning could scale beyond simple gridworlds to complex real-world environments if effective mechanisms exist for translating human judgment into learning signals. The approach points toward agents that mature beyond their teachers while retaining safety properties, and raises further questions about how to formalize parenting-like interventions, ensure human oversight remains effective as agent capability grows, and achieve generalization to environments beyond human training experience.
